mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Mexico on?
mapQuestions.answer: North America. Mexico is the southern part of North America, sitting between the USA and Central America 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Mexico?
mapQuestions.answer: Mexico City (Ciudad de México). It sits 2,250 metres up in a high valley and is one of the biggest cities in the world, with about 22 million people in the metro area.

mapQuestions.question: What two oceans / seas touch Mexico?
mapQuestions.answer: The Pacific Ocean on the west, and the Gulf of Mexico and Caribbean Sea on the east 🌊

mapQuestions.question: What two countries border Mexico?
mapQuestions.answer: The USA to the north, Guatemala (and Belize) to the south.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Mexico?
mapQuestions.answer: Pico de Orizaba, 5,636 metres, a volcano east of Mexico City. It is the third-highest mountain in North America.

foods.name: Tacos al pastor
foods.description: Marinated pork sliced from a spinning vertical spit, served on small corn tortillas with onion, coriander, pineapple and salsa. Invented in Mexico City by Lebanese immigrants.

foods.name: Guacamole + tortilla chips
foods.description: Fresh mashed avocado with lime, salt, onion, coriander and chilli, scooped up with crispy fried tortilla triangles called totopos.

foods.name: Elote
foods.description: Charcoal-grilled corn on the cob, slathered with mayonnaise, cotija cheese, chilli powder and lime. Sold on every Mexican street corner.

foods.name: Mole
foods.description: A complex sauce with sometimes 30+ ingredients including chillies, nuts, seeds, spices and (in mole poblano) dark chocolate. Slow-cooked for a whole day, served over chicken.

foods.name: Churros + chocolate
foods.description: Long crispy ridged donut sticks dusted with cinnamon sugar, dipped into a cup of thick hot chocolate. Best as a late-night snack.

foods.name: Tamales
foods.description: Steamed pockets of corn dough filled with chicken, pork, mole or sweet things, wrapped in a corn husk or banana leaf. Eaten as breakfast on the street.

scavengerItems.label: A real cactus growing in the wild or in a garden
A sugar skull or Day of the Dead decoration in a shop window
A piñata hanging up at a party or in a market
A mariachi musician (trumpet, violin, big guitar)
Papel picado (cut-paper flag-banners) strung above a street
A Lucha Libre wrestler mask in a shop or museum
A pile of bright red and green chillies at a market stall
A bright woven blanket or rug on display
A church with a tall stone bell tower in a town square
The Mexican flag with the eagle eating a snake on a cactus
An iguana sunning itself on a wall or in a tree
Aztec or Maya stone carvings on a building or museum piece

geoMatches.description: The capital, built on top of the ancient Aztec city of Tenochtitlán. 22 million people, 2,250m above sea level, museums, food stalls, and the giant Zócalo square.
A colourful colonial city in the south. Home to 16 indigenous groups, mole sauces, mezcal, weavers, and the best street food in Mexico.
A massive Maya stone pyramid in the Yucatán jungle. On the spring and autumn equinox, the shadow of a serpent appears to slide down its staircase.
The Caribbean coast of the Yucatán. White sand, turquoise water, Maya ruins right on the beach, and underwater "cenotes" (limestone sinkholes) you can swim in.
A network of canyons in the northern Sierra Madre mountains, bigger and deeper than the Grand Canyon. Home to the Rarámuri (Tarahumara) running people.
The long Pacific peninsula in the north-west. Desert one side, ocean the other. Gray whales come here to give birth every winter.

senses.smell: Fresh corn tortillas cooking on a comal, lime, charcoal grills smoking on the corner, simmering mole, ripe papaya at a market, marigolds in November…
senses.hear: Mariachi trumpets, the slap of dough being patted into tortillas, "¡Hola!" "¡Buen provecho!", motorbikes weaving, church bells, distant kids playing fútbol…
senses.taste: Hot tortilla off a hot iron plate, salsa stinging your tongue, sweet cinnamon horchata, ice-cold Coca-Cola in a glass bottle, melting brigadeiro, smoky mezcal…
senses.feel: Hot sun, cool tile floor in a courtyard, sticky sweat on the back of your neck, dusty mountain air, fresh ocean spray on a Pacific beach…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are tortillas everywhere?
stories.body: Corn was first farmed in Mexico around 9,000 years ago, from a wild grass called teosinte. Ancient Mexicans worked out a clever trick called nixtamalisation, soaking dried corn kernels in water with a little lime (the mineral, not the fruit). This makes the corn easier to grind, more nutritious, and gives a tortilla its proper smell and stretch. Mexicans eat about 8 kg of tortillas per person per month. Tortillas are still patted by hand in some villages, and every region has its own taco — pastor in Mexico City, carnitas in Michoacán, cochinita pibil in the Yucatán. A taco is basically anything you can wrap in a fresh hot tortilla.

stories.question: Why is Day of the Dead a happy day?
stories.body: Every year on November 1st and 2nd, Mexican families celebrate Día de los Muertos — the Day of the Dead. They build colourful altars (ofrendas) covered with photos of relatives who have died, marigold flowers, candles, sugar skulls, and the favourite foods and drinks of the dead. The idea is NOT sad: it is the one time of year the spirits of loved ones come back to visit. Families have picnics in cemeteries. Children paint their faces as skeletons. The whole festival is colourful and full of music. UNESCO protects it as world heritage. Disney/Pixar even made a film about it called Coco.
stories.thinkingQuestion: In Mexico, the dead are celebrated, not just mourned. What do you think is a good way to remember people who have died?

stories.question: Why is there an eagle eating a snake on Mexico's flag?
stories.body: According to an ancient Aztec legend, the wandering Aztec people were told by their gods to search until they spotted an eagle perched on a cactus, holding a snake in its beak. There they should build their new capital. After many years of wandering they finally saw this exact scene on a small island in a lake in central Mexico, in the year 1325. They drained part of the lake, built a city on top called Tenochtitlán, and ruled an empire from it for the next 200 years. When the Spanish destroyed the empire in 1521, they built their own capital on top, modern Mexico City. The lake is mostly gone now, but the eagle, the cactus and the snake are still on every Mexican flag.

stories.question: How did Spain conquer the Aztec Empire?
stories.body: When the Spanish conqueror Hernán Cortés first saw Tenochtitlán in 1519, his men could not believe what they were looking at. The Aztec capital sat on an island in the middle of a lake, connected to land by three giant causeways. It had more people than any city in Spain. It had floating vegetable gardens called chinampas, aqueducts bringing fresh mountain water, and dazzling pyramid-temples. With just 600 Spanish soldiers — but tens of thousands of local allies who hated Aztec rule, plus smallpox that they unknowingly brought with them — Cortés brought down the empire in two years. The disease killed millions of people who had never met it before. The Spanish destroyed most of Tenochtitlán and built Mexico City on top, using stones from the old temples for the new churches.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A 600-man army defeated an empire of millions, partly thanks to allies and partly thanks to disease. Why do you think no Aztec story is taught in school in most countries?

stories.question: Who lives in Mexico now?
stories.body: Mexico today is "mestizo", a blend of indigenous and Spanish heritage. Around 130 million people live there, speaking Spanish plus 68 recognised indigenous languages (Nahuatl, Maya, Zapotec, Mixtec and many more). Every Mexican state has its own food, music, dance and identity. Mexican popular culture has become a global force: telenovelas, reggaeton, lucha libre wrestling, mariachi, Frida Kahlo paintings, Día de los Muertos. The country is also one of the most biodiverse on Earth, sitting at the meeting point of North American and tropical Central American species. About a quarter of the country is desert, but it also has rainforest, snowy volcanoes, coral reefs and one of the world's largest cave systems.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you have eaten in Mexico so far?
How spicy was the salsa? Survival rating out of 10?
The colours here — bright walls, bright clothes, bright food — how is it making you feel?
Mexicans say "ahorita" to mean "right now, but maybe later". What do you make of that?
Have you spotted a piñata, Day of the Dead decoration, or mariachi band yet?
500 years ago the place we're standing was an Aztec city. What might still be under your feet?

wordSearch.hint: Mexican foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Iguana
animals.description: Big green or grey lizards basking on warm walls and rocks in Yucatán ruins.

animals.name: Mercado cat
animals.description: Sleek market cats around every Mexican mercado, fed by stallholders.

animals.name: Sea turtle
animals.description: Olive ridley, green and hawksbill turtles nest on Pacific and Caribbean beaches.

animals.name: Crested caracara
animals.description: A striking black-and-white raptor with a crest, common in rural Mexico.

animals.name: Monarch butterfly
animals.description: In November–March, millions migrate to fir forests in Michoacán. UNESCO listed.

animals.name: Grey whale
animals.description: Birthing calves in Baja California lagoons every winter. Boats can come close.
